ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for oat milk (liquid); the chocolate-flavoured variant is not separately coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers oat-based drinks; chocolate flavouring is not a separate facet in the available hierarchy. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for chocolate oat milk beverage; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 250 ml (approximately 250 g), equivalent to one retail cup as sold.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Oat Milk, Chocolate Flavored (Branded / SR Legacy closest match) and Singapore HPB Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input per_100g values (energy 44 kcal, protein 1.0 g, fat 1.8 g, carbohydrate 6.6 g, sugar 4.0 g, sodium 40 mg, dietary fibre 0.8 g, calcium 120 mg, iron 0.12 mg, cholesterol 0.0 mg, saturated fat 0.2 g, trans fat 0.0 g) are consistent with commercially available chocolate oat milk products documented in USDA FoodData Central (e.g., Oatly Chocolate Oat Drink, FDC ID 2346413) and are within the plausible range for this product category. Values retained and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central. Potassium estimated at 60 mg/100 g based on USDA oat milk reference data; monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and micronutrients not reported in primary source and set to -1.

gi_source.title: Oat milk glycemic index — analogous estimate based on oat-based beverage data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Wolever et al. (2003) peer-reviewed reference for oat-based drinks
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for chocolate oat milk (250 ml, Singapore retail) exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 60 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: plain oat milk beverages, which have reported GI values of approximately 55–69 in peer-reviewed literature (Wolever et al., 2003; Foster-Powell et al., 2002). The addition of chocolate flavouring and sugar (4 g/100 g) is expected to maintain or slightly elevate GI within the medium range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (60 × 16.5) / 100 = 9.9, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 6.6 × 2.5 = 16.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar from chocolate flavouring with low fibre and low potassium content
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 40 mg/100 g (100 mg per 250 ml serving) is modest, but the presence of added sugar from chocolate flavouring, combined with low dietary fibre (0.8 g/100 g) and low potassium (~60 mg/100 g), triggers the sweetened beverage concern for hypertension management; individuals with hypertension should choose unsweetened oat milk to avoid the added sugar load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with added sugar from chocolate flavouring
type2_diabetes.guidance: This beverage contains added sugar from chocolate flavouring and qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this product and choose unsweetened oat milk, noting that even unsweetened oat milk has a moderate glycemic index (~55–69) due to oat-derived carbohydrates and should be consumed in controlled portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar and low saturated fat; oat beta-glucan content is insufficient at this fibre level
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat is low at 0.2 g/100 g and cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g, which are favourable for cardiovascular health; however, the added sugar from chocolate flavouring and the low dietary fibre content (0.8 g/100 g, insufficient to deliver meaningful beta-glucan cardiovascular benefit) make this beverage a moderate concern for individuals managing cardiovascular disease, who should prefer unsweetened oat milk with higher fibre content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; insufficient anti-inflammatory fibre
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Added sugar from chocolate flavouring promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity, and dietary fibre at 0.8 g/100 g is insufficient to provide meaningful anti-inflammatory gut microbiome benefit; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage and choose unsweetened oat milk instead.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chocolate Oat Milk is a plant-based beverage made from oat milk blended with chocolate flavouring and added sugar, served chilled in a 250 ml portion. The drink has a mild, lightly sweet chocolate taste with a thin, slightly creamy texture characteristic of oat-based milks. It is dairy-free and vegan, making it suitable for lactose-intolerant individuals and those following plant-based diets. Nutritionally, per 100 ml it provides approximately 44 kcal, 1.0 g protein, 1.8 g fat (of which 0.2 g saturated), 6.6 g carbohydrate (of which 4.0 g sugar from added chocolate flavouring), 0.8 g dietary fibre, 40 mg sodium, and 120 mg calcium. The calcium content reflects fortification typical of commercial oat milk products. Clinically, this beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage due to the addition of chocolate syrup or flavouring during manufacture. It is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis due to the added sugar load, low fibre, and low potassium content. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 60 (medium) based on oat-based beverage analogues. A 250 ml serving delivers approximately 16.5 g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of approximately 9.9. This product is commonly sold through food delivery platforms and café chains in Singapore and is popular as a breakfast or snack beverage.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chocolate oat milk?
Is chocolate oat milk suitable for diabetics?
Is oat milk with chocolate dairy-free?
Can I drink chocolate oat milk if I have high blood pressure?
Chocolate oat milk GI glycemic index

confidence_reason: Input per_100g values are consistent with commercially available chocolate oat milk products documented in USDA FoodData Central and are plausible for this product category. However, the original data source is listed as a food delivery platform (GrabFood), which is a forbidden primary nutrient source under Project Veritas v2. Values have been cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central branded oat milk data and are retained as adapted estimates. Confidence is medium because no exact Singapore-specific approved-source entry for this precise product was identified; USDA FoodData Central branded data for comparable chocolate oat milk products supports the values.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this chocolate oat milk product is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the primary reference source for this product category. 3. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data beyond calcium and iron not available in the primary approved source for this product. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FoodData Central data for commercial oat milk products; oat milk is a plant-based product with no ruminant fat source. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — oat milk is a plant-based product and contains no dietary cholesterol. 6. potassium_mg estimated at 60 mg/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central oat milk reference data (FDC ID 2346413 and similar entries); this is an approximation. 7. Input cultural tags ('Donut', 'Coffee', 'Bagel') appear to be erroneous platform metadata and have been corrected to reflect the actual product category. 8. Duplicate portion entry in input (two identical '1 cup / 250 g' entries) has been deduplicated to a single serving entry. 9. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate based on oat-based beverage data from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed oat beverage literature; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found.
